## Model selection is a workflow choice

Model selection means choosing between competing modelling workflows, not just between
two fitted coefficient tables. In `TEM 0052`, the candidate choices might differ in
features, regularization strength, preprocessing, or even the full algorithm family.

The question is therefore practical: which workflow is most likely to generalize well
to new data?

## Information criteria as a fast complexity penalty

Information criteria such as AIC and BIC combine goodness of fit with a penalty for
model complexity.

- `AIC` is usually more prediction-oriented and tends to penalize complexity less
  aggressively.
- `BIC` applies a stronger penalty and will often prefer smaller models when sample
  size grows.

For linear or likelihood-based models, these criteria give a quick screening rule. In
the legacy course material, lasso model selection on the diabetes data is a good
example of how complexity penalties can shrink a large candidate set quickly.

::: {.teacher-only}
If students already know ridge and lasso, pause here and ask whether an information
criterion is selecting coefficients directly or selecting among fitted workflows.
:::

## Cross-validation as the default course rule

Cross-validation is the default selection rule when we want a direct estimate of
out-of-sample performance.

1. Split the data into repeated train/validation folds.
2. Fit each candidate workflow inside the same resampling design.
3. Compare mean validation performance together with variation across folds.

This makes the selection rule visible and reproducible. Students can see not only
which workflow performs best on average, but also whether that conclusion is stable.

## What to report

In this course, model selection reports should usually include:

- the candidate workflows that were compared,
- the resampling protocol,
- the chosen metric,
- the selected model or hyper-parameter,
- and one short explanation of why the final choice is defensible.

That reporting discipline matters just as much as the final score. A reproducible
selection rule is more valuable than an opaque “best model” claim.
